section.title: Warning Against Idleness
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from doctrine to daily life. He asks for prayer for the spread of the word and for deliverance, then confidently commends the faithful Lord. The heart of the section is a firm correction of certain idle, disorderly members who have stopped working and become busybodies, over against Paul's own example of hard labor and the plain rule that the unwilling to work should not eat. He closes with careful instructions for loving discipline of the disobedient.
section.thesis: The faithful Lord who strengthens His people also calls them to orderly, productive lives; so the church must lovingly correct idle, disorderly members, hold up the apostolic example of self-supporting labor, refuse to grow weary in doing good, and discipline the disobedient not as enemies but as brothers.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the request for prayer and the confidence in the faithful Lord who guards and directs His people; the command against idleness, grounded in Paul's example and the rule that the unwilling to work should not eat; and the closing instructions to keep doing good and to discipline the disobedient as brothers, not enemies.

units.label: Prayer and the faithful Lord
units.note: for the word to spread and the church to be guarded

verses.text: Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may spread quickly and be held in honor, just as it was with you.
And pray that we may be delivered from wicked and evil men; for not everyone holds to the faith.
But the Lord is {{pistos|faithful}}, and He will strengthen you and guard you from the evil one.
And we have confidence in the Lord that you are doing and will continue to do what we command.
May the Lord direct your hearts into God's love and Christ's perseverance.

units.label: The command against idleness
units.note: the apostolic example and the rule about work

verses.text: Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to keep away from any brother who leads an {{ataktos|undisciplined}} life that is not in keeping with the {{paradosis|tradition}} you received from us.
For you yourselves know how you ought to imitate us, because we were not undisciplined among you,
nor did we eat anyone's food without paying for it. Instead, in labor and toil, we worked night and day so that we would not be a burden to any of you.
Not that we lack this right, but we wanted to offer ourselves as an example for you to imitate.
For even while we were with you, we gave you this command: "If anyone is unwilling to {{ergazomai|work}}, he shall not eat."
For we hear that some of you are leading undisciplined lives, accomplishing nothing, but being {{periergazomai|busybodies}}.
We command and urge such people by our Lord Jesus Christ to begin working quietly to earn their own living.

units.label: Do good and discipline as brothers

verses.text: But as for you, brothers, do not grow weary in well-doing.
Take note of anyone who does not obey the instructions we have given in this letter. Do not associate with him, so that he may be ashamed.
Yet do not regard him as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, reliable. Paul turns from the faithlessness of wicked men in verse 2 to the faithfulness of the Lord in verse 3: not everyone holds to the faith, but the Lord is faithful. God's dependable character is the ground for the promise that He will strengthen and guard His people from the evil one.

paradosis.gloss: Tradition, that which is handed over or passed on. As in chapter two, it means the authoritative apostolic teaching, here including the example and instruction about work that Paul delivered in person. The idle members are living out of step with this received tradition, and so are to be corrected.

ataktos.gloss: Disorderly, undisciplined, idle; originally a military term for a soldier out of rank or a line that has broken formation. It describes members who have abandoned the orderly duty of daily work. The word covers more than laziness: it is a life out of step, disrupting the church's order and burdening others.

ergazomai.gloss: To work, labor, be busy at a task. It anchors Paul's rule that the one unwilling to work should not eat, and his call to the idle to work quietly and earn their own bread. Honest labor is treated as the normal, dignified calling of the believer, and Paul's own night-and-day toil is the model.

periergazomai.gloss: To be a busybody, to meddle, to be busy about the wrong things. Paul coins a deliberate wordplay: the idle are not working, ergazomai, but working around, periergazomai, meddling in others' affairs. Idleness is not neutral; the energy withdrawn from honest labor is spent disturbing the community.

basic.context: Paul closes the letter with practical matters. He asks the church to pray that the gospel would run and that he would be rescued from hostile men, then reassures them that the Lord is faithful to strengthen and guard them. The main concern is a group in the church who have stopped working, are living off others, and have become meddlers. Paul points to his own example of paying his way by hard work, repeats his rule that those unwilling to work should not eat, and tells the idle to get back to quiet, self-supporting labor. Finally he explains how to discipline those who refuse to obey: firmly, but as brothers, not enemies.
basic.hermeneutics: The rule that the unwilling to work should not eat targets those who will not work, not those who cannot; it is not a denial of care for the genuinely needy, whom Scripture everywhere commands us to help. The disorderly problem may have been fed by a distorted view of Christ's return, as if imminence excused ordinary duties. The discipline Paul prescribes aims at restoration, shame that leads to repentance, not rejection.
basic.originalLanguages: The Lord is pistos, faithful, over against faithless men. The problem is the ataktos life, a military image of a soldier out of rank, a disorderly idleness out of step with the paradosis, the apostolic tradition. Paul's rule turns on ergazomai, to work: the unwilling to work should not eat. He coins a pointed wordplay in verse 11: the idle are not working, ergazomai, but periergazomai, working around, meddling as busybodies.
basic.typology: Paul's self-supporting labor and refusal to be a burden reflects the pattern of honest work woven through Scripture from the garden onward, where God gives humanity the dignity of labor. The call not to grow weary in well-doing anticipates the harvest promised to those who persevere in doing good.
basic.christ: The commands are given in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ and by His authority, showing that even ordinary matters of work and order fall under His lordship. Paul prays that the Lord would direct their hearts into God's love and Christ's perseverance, so that the patient endurance of Christ Himself becomes the pattern and power for the church's faithful, orderly life.
basic.god: God is faithful. Against a backdrop of wicked men and internal disorder, the Lord is the one who strengthens His people, guards them from the evil one, and directs their hearts into His love. He is a God of order, not chaos, who dignifies honest work and calls His people to steady, productive, loving lives.
basic.crossReferences: First Thessalonians 4 already urged this church to work with their hands and lead quiet lives. Ephesians 4 tells the former thief to work honestly so as to have something to share. And Galatians 6 says do not grow weary in doing good, for we will reap a harvest, echoed almost exactly here.
basic.oneStory: The gospel produces not only right belief but ordered, generous, hardworking lives. The faithful Lord who guards His people also shapes a community marked by honest labor, mutual care, perseverance in good, and loving discipline, a foretaste of the ordered new creation where God's people serve Him without weariness.

context.literary: Finally in verse 1 signals the letter's closing section. It moves from prayer request, verses 1 and 2, to confidence in the faithful Lord, verses 3 to 5, then to the sustained command against idleness, verses 6 to 12, built on Paul's personal example and his remembered rule, and closes with instructions to persevere in good and to discipline the disobedient, verses 13 to 15. The repeated word command marks its authority.
context.historical: In the Greco-Roman world some looked down on manual labor and relied on patrons, and a few in the church may have used the expectation of Christ's soon return to justify quitting work. Paul, a tentmaker, had deliberately paid his own way in Thessalonica to model self-support and to keep the gospel free of charge, giving him the moral standing to correct the idle now.
context.canonical: The passage stands with the New Testament's consistent teaching on the dignity of work, the duty of self-support, generosity to the truly needy, and orderly church discipline aimed at restoration. Its close echoes of 1 Thessalonians and Galatians show a settled apostolic ethic of diligent, loving, community-minded living.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To secure prayer for the gospel's advance, to reassure the church of the Lord's faithful protection, and above all to correct the disorderly idlers by apostolic command and example, calling them back to quiet, self-supporting work, while guiding the church to persevere in good and to discipline the disobedient in a restorative, brotherly way.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's account of his own labor, verses 7 to 9, is a description that carries prescriptive force as a deliberate example to imitate. The rule of verse 10 and the commands of verses 6, 12, and 14 are prescriptive. The prescriptions target the willfully idle within the church family; they are not a general economic policy indifferent to the poor and unable.

debates.question: How should we understand the rule, if anyone is unwilling to work, he shall not eat?

views.label: It addresses the unwilling, not the unable
views.case: The wording targets those who choose not to work; Scripture elsewhere commands generous care for the poor, the sick, widows, and orphans, so this is no excuse to neglect the genuinely needy.

views.label: It is a principle of dignity and responsibility, not a harsh maxim
views.case: Paul aims to restore idlers to the dignity of honest labor and to protect the church from being exploited; the rule is corrective love within the family, not a cold welfare rule for society at large.

debates.bearing: Read rightly, the verse upholds both the dignity of work and the duty of compassion, correcting the idle without hardening the church against the truly helpless.

debates.question: What kind of discipline does Paul prescribe in verses 14 and 15?

views.label: Restorative withdrawal aimed at repentance
views.case: The church is to note and not associate with the disobedient so that he may be ashamed and repent, while still admonishing him as a brother; the goal is restoration, not rejection.

views.label: A limited social distancing, not total excommunication
views.case: Do not associate is real but measured, and explicitly stops short of treating him as an enemy; it is a family discipline that keeps the door open for return.

debates.bearing: Church discipline here is firm yet loving, meant to reclaim a wandering brother, holding together accountability and hope of reconciliation.

study.originalLanguages: Paul sets the faithlessness of wicked men against the character of the Lord, who is pistos, faithful, and therefore strong to guard His people from the evil one. The problem in the church is the ataktos life, a term borrowed from the army for a soldier who has left his rank or a line that has broken formation, picturing idleness as disorder that breaks step with the paradosis, the apostolic tradition. The rule of verse 10 rests on ergazomai, to work: the one unwilling to work should not eat, and the idle are urged to return to quiet, self-supporting ergazomai. Verse 11 lands a pointed pun that the English can only hint at: they are not working, ergazomai, but periergazomai, working all around, busybodies, showing that idleness does not leave energy unspent but redirects it into meddling that harms the community.

typology.fulfillment: Reflects the goodness of work given at creation, when God set humanity to tend the garden, now upheld in the church's calling to self-supporting, God-honoring labor.
Anticipates the promised harvest for those who persevere in doing good, the reward of the age to come for faithful endurance now.

study.christ: Christ's lordship reaches into the everyday. Paul issues his commands in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ and urges the idle by that same name, so that work, order, and discipline are matters of obedience to Christ, not mere pragmatism. He prays that the Lord would direct their hearts into God's love and Christ's perseverance, making the patient steadfastness of Jesus the pattern and source of the church's diligence. The faithful Lord who guards them is the risen Christ, present and active among His people.
study.god: God is faithful and orderly. Surrounded by wicked men and troubled by internal disorder, the church is pointed to the Lord who is faithful, who strengthens and guards them from the evil one, and who directs their hearts into His love. This God dignifies honest work, opposes idleness and meddling, and forms a community of steady goodness, mutual responsibility, and restorative love, reflecting His own faithful and well-ordered character.

crossReferences.note: Aspire to live quietly, mind your own affairs, and work with your hands, the same counsel now enforced against the idle.
Let the thief work honestly so he may have something to share; honest labor for the sake of generosity.
Let us not grow weary in doing good, for in due season we will reap, almost exactly echoed in verse 13.
The pattern of noting, confronting, and, if needed, distancing from an unrepentant brother, aimed at his restoration.

study.oneStory: This passage shows the gospel shaping ordinary life. The faithful Lord who advances His word and guards His people also forms a community of honest labor, patient goodness, and loving accountability. Against idleness, exploitation, and meddling, the church is called to ordered, generous living and to discipline that restores rather than destroys, a small but real picture of the new creation where God's redeemed people serve Him in love without ever growing weary.

quiz.q: What does Paul ask the church to pray for in verses 1 and 2?

options.text: That the word of the Lord would spread and be honored, and that he would be delivered from wicked men.
options.note: Verses 1 and 2 request prayer for the gospel's advance and for deliverance.

options.text: That the church would gain political power in the city.
options.note: Paul prays for the word's spread and his protection, not for political power.

options.text: That all persecution would immediately end forever.
options.note: He asks for deliverance from evil men, but the focus is the word running freely.

options.text: That the idle members would simply be ignored.
options.note: The prayer requests concern the gospel and deliverance; the idle are addressed later, not ignored.

quiz.q: What comfort does Paul give in verse 3, over against faithless men?

options.text: The Lord is faithful and will strengthen and guard the church from the evil one.
options.note: Verse 3 contrasts faithless men with the faithful Lord who guards His people.

options.text: The church must simply defend itself by its own strength.
options.note: It is the Lord who strengthens and guards them, not their own strength alone.

options.text: God will remove every difficult person from their lives at once.
options.note: The promise is protection and strengthening, not the instant removal of all opponents.

options.text: There is no real protection against evil.
options.note: The verse plainly promises the faithful Lord will guard them from the evil one.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the problem with certain members in verses 6 and 11?

options.text: They are living undisciplined, idle lives, not working but becoming busybodies.
options.note: Verses 6 and 11 describe disorderly idleness and meddling.

options.text: They are working too hard and neglecting worship.
options.note: The problem is idleness, not overwork.

options.text: They are giving away too much of their money.
options.note: The issue is refusing to work and meddling, not excessive generosity.

options.text: They are traveling to plant new churches.
options.note: They are accomplishing nothing and meddling, not doing missionary work.

quiz.q: What example did Paul set while he was with them, according to verses 7 to 9?

options.text: He worked night and day and paid his own way, so as not to burden anyone.
options.note: Verses 7 to 9 recount Paul's self-supporting labor as a deliberate example.

options.text: He demanded to be fully supported by the church.
options.note: Though he had that right, he chose to pay his own way as a model.

options.text: He avoided all work to devote himself only to prayer.
options.note: He explicitly worked in labor and toil among them.

options.text: He lived off the wealth of rich patrons.
options.note: He deliberately did not eat anyone's food without paying, unlike relying on patrons.

quiz.q: What rule had Paul given, according to verse 10?

options.text: If anyone is unwilling to work, he shall not eat.
options.note: Verse 10 states the rule aimed at the willfully idle.

options.text: No believer should ever earn a wage.
options.note: The rule commends work, not the avoidance of earning.

options.text: Only the wealthy are required to work.
options.note: The rule applies to any who are unwilling to work, not only the wealthy.

options.text: The needy and disabled must fend entirely for themselves.
options.note: The rule targets the unwilling, not those genuinely unable; Scripture commands care for the needy.

quiz.q: How are believers to treat a disobedient brother, according to verses 14 and 15?

options.text: Note him and withhold association so he feels shame, yet admonish him as a brother, not an enemy.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 prescribe restorative discipline that still treats him as a brother.

options.text: Cut him off permanently and treat him as an enemy.
options.note: Paul explicitly says not to regard him as an enemy but to admonish him as a brother.

options.text: Ignore his behavior entirely and say nothing.
options.note: They are to take note and admonish him, not ignore the disobedience.

options.text: Punish him harshly to make an example of him.
options.note: The aim is loving, restorative shame that leads to repentance, not harsh punishment.

questions: Paul turns from faithless men to the faithful Lord who strengthens and guards His people. Where do you need to fix your eyes on the Lord's faithfulness rather than on the unreliable people around you?

The passage treats honest work as a dignified calling and even models paying one's own way. How do you view your daily work, and how might seeing it as service to Christ change your attitude toward it?
Paul warns that idleness turns into meddling; energy withdrawn from good work gets spent on being a busybody. Where might restlessness or avoidance be tempting you to meddle rather than to serve?
The command is not to grow weary in doing good. In what area of well-doing are you tired right now, and what would help you keep going?
Church discipline here is firm yet aims to restore a brother, never to destroy an enemy. How can you hold together honesty about sin and genuine love when someone close to you goes astray?

translationNotes.note: Undisciplined translates ataktos, a military term for a soldier out of rank; it denotes disorderly idleness, a life out of step with the received apostolic tradition.
If anyone is unwilling to work, he shall not eat concerns those who refuse to work, not those unable to; the wider biblical witness commands generous care for the genuinely needy.
The Greek plays on words that English can only approximate: they are not working (ergazomenous) but working around, that is, being busybodies (periergazomenous).

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Thessalonians 3:1-15. No em or en dashes appear in this passage; the quotation in verse 10 uses straight quotation marks. All fifteen verses covered contiguously.
